#10 The flight to Japan

Areas discussed: Japan, Tokyo, Suginami City, Meguro City, Narita International Airport, Shibuya Crossing, Sangenjaya

December 13, 2023 1 hour 45 minutes

All transcripts are machine generated.

Transcript

Alright, I'm recording this episode the same day as the previous episode, so I was at that cool park with the squat toilets and the walk down the Kenda River. So it turns out there were a whole bunch of subways all over the place. Plenty of options I could have taken to get home. But every time I would see a train go by, they were extremely packed with people. And it's not that late.

Like I put on a second layer. I put on like a thermal shirt briefly. But then once the sun set, all the wind stopped. And it's not cold anymore. Now it's very nice.

Tiny little breeze on my bare t-shirt at arms feels good. And I've also noticed, although this is nothing to do with Japan. This is just me in general. But I kind of forgot about this. That when the sun's going down, it triggers my brain to feel tired. It's like that. Sun's going down. Day's over. And I feel so sluggish. Like it's just dragging me down. This used to always happen to me in Toronto and stuff. I think like, oh man, I can't go meet people tonight. I am dying. But then once the sun goes down, it's like a new day. It's like I'm in a second day. I don't feel tired at all. It's like, oh, it's nighttime now. New zone in the day. New time in the day.

So I got this seat where I was sitting by the aisle, which is nice because you can just get up and go to the washroom without having to bother people, which that's almost worth it alone. So it was very disturbing to me how much my toes were hurting and trying different stretches and stuff. I had no idea why this all of a sudden started happening and nothing I was doing was helping. But as I was researching stuff, it seems like bunions were the problem, which is one of those terms I had heard. I thought they were just like bumps on your feet, which they kind of are, but it turns out it's because it just happens naturally with some people. And I have a long history of feet problems. I had to wear these weird devices on my feet when I was a kid to try to ameliorate my flat feet. Didn't work though, so I have flat feet. And I always had to wear special inserts in my shoes. But I guess bunions, it's because your feet are supposed to, your toes are supposed to split out, although if your feet split out too much, because you never wear shoes, apparently that's bad too. 35:00 But they're supposed to split out to a certain degree just to give you balance. Your big toes are supposed to point toward each other basically so that you have more area, because so much of the pressure of your body goes on your big toes. But modern shoes for some reason have very narrow fronts a lot of the time. So there's no areas and it makes your toes get pushed away from each other. And because they're bent in that weird direction, it does cause a bump, but the bump is like the bone of your foot, poking out. And like once it happens, it's quite irreversible without surgery. It seems awful. So in my case, it was a bit from shoes, possibly, but my shoes actually backs. They didn't have quite a wide toe box. Although after this, the next pair of shoes I buy, I'm going to deliberately go by the biggest ones I can find, the most toe room. So I think it's mostly just a natural phenomenon with me, but maybe a bit of both. But once I found out that that was it, the way I tested it is I balled up socks and I put the socks between my big toe and my next toe over at night. Because I would be like, that's the thing. It wasn't even just walking that would make my toes hurt. I'd wake up in the morning with them hurting. Something I was doing at night was making them hurt. So I balled the socks up between my toes and sometimes they fell out, they didn't always stay in. But I did notice, noticeably, the next day my toes hurt less. I'm like, all right, I'm on to something here. So I got two different devices. For the daytime, the ones I'm wearing right now are just these little rubber toe rings. It's like a ring you put around your big toe that has a spacer between the big toe and the next toe over. So, you know, they're a little awkward, and as I got them a new rhythm around a bit, they get in weird positions and can cause some pain in and of themselves sometimes. But overall, they fantastically help because they just keep the toes separate. They keep the big toe aligned more or less like it's supposed to be instead of getting pushed inward. And then at night, I've got more elaborate ones that I put on that are like a whole weird brace that I put on each foot. That when I was just stomping around my apartment back in Canada, it's like, so what, I'm wearing these weird toe things. It's a little weirder now, like this Airbnb or the one in Montreal where it's more communal and I see other people. But I think like they say in Shawshank, how often do you look at a man's shoes and they kind of just look like weird slippers if you don't look too close. And I'm just like, whatever, I'm just wearing these things. They make a little weird clippity clop when I walk. I'm like, like, cell from portal, those weird things she's got on her feet or a protest from Starcraft. That's what I think of. I've got these weird feet, but they also hold my toes apart at night, hold my big toe away from my next toe over. Just to help sort of train your body to trend that way. And I have a feeling I'll have to wear both of these things forever, but I don't mind. I like the fact that I've been walking around for all the time in Montreal and all the time here and probably a week back in Canada, it's been over a month now. And just, yeah, it's like amazing. The toe pain is so incredibly reduced, 98% reduced. And it's just so rare for one of these medical problems to have such a clean solution. Lord knows I don't have that for my bad knee or my bad shoulder or my bad cyst buried in my abdomen. So, yeah, holy shit, right? Because that would have just, man, this would have been such a bummer if I couldn't walk around because my feet were just hurting all the time. And my toes were in pain. I guess I would have to do a lot more subway stuff. And a lot more of what I'm doing right now, a lot more sitting and contemplating Japan rather than actually moving through it. I'm sure that's common. Every once in a while, I see like a really old person here walking along like bent over with canes. And I feel very deep sympathy for those people because, man, I mean, I don't know, my grandmother's both lived to be fairly old, but one of them really old, my grandmother on my dad's side was 101. And in both cases, I was shocked how spry they were for such old people, but some old people yikes, like that is not the case. Something went terribly wrong. And I was going to say, hopefully that doesn't happen to me, but both my grandfather's died quite young. My dad was only 70 when he died. I'm probably just going to die, so I guess that solves that problem.

Alright, let's get moving again. Oh yeah, feels better on the old shoulders, maybe I just got to take more breaks. I didn't get to take as much of a break at that park as I wanted to because I've never really noticed that much mosquito activity in Japan, but I know they do have them. 40:00 I mean, where I'm from, the east coast of Canada, the mosquitoes are fucking insane, so compared to that, nothing seems so bad around here, but that park in particular. A lot of mosquitoes, so I didn't stay very long. I've just been walking, walking like a fool because I do just need to take breaks a little more often.

So yeah, thanks to the travel guide, things have gone better than I could even ask for, really no complaints. So that being said, let me complain. So the flight was already a bit late because Calgary is a frozen death pit, you know, it was below freezing. They had to de-ice the plane, there was snow everywhere, so that delayed us.

But basically, I mean, I think I still got there like 5.30 and the check-in thing for this hostel wasn't until 10. And I mean, ultimately, that didn't matter at all what time, but it was just that the guy trying to be helpful. A lot of the problems with this hostel are weird because the guy is really trying. He's really trying. And it's just a weird example of how you can put all the effort in the world into things, but sometimes it just doesn't work out. It just is a problem anyway. But this is one example of that, as I got a message from the guy at the airport.

Basically just saying, like, not that I had to be there by 10 o'clock, but he could only help me until 10 o'clock. And I saw in the reviews for this place some people that said they did have problems getting in initially.

And this guy would be around to help out till 10. After 10, it's cut off and no more. And I'll tell you why. Normally it'd be like whatever. You know, I kind of felt that way in Montreal, too, of like the shore, a lot of door codes. I sure hope this goes okay, but it all went okay. But the thing here is, is God is so elaborate. Again, especially once I got to this place and I realized how just this little tiny, rinky, dink house, just packed full of stuff. But you have to go on to this website. It's all fancy fans that looks like a four-star hotel website, feeling all your info and do all your stuff, because you do need Airbnb and Japan are treated a bit like hotels in the sense that you're supposed to get all the info of people that are staying, their passport numbers, where they're from, where they were just at, where they're going next, get you out of here.

So you did all that. And then you got a check-in code with offers as well for all the amenities. Do you want a towel? Do you want this? Do you want that? There's only $2 for this or $1 for that, which is just, that's a little annoying. When places do that, it's like just give me a towel. Don't charge me $2 for a towel. I won't pay it. I will never pay you $2 for a towel. You can give me a towel. I'm very self-sufficient. I would like a towel, but if you don't give me one, it'll be a cold day in hell. Pay you for a towel that you should just give me. But from there, once you're filling all the stuff, you get your little code, a number and a QR code. And then also, sticking with the idea of helpful but not at the same time. This guy had sent a YouTube video a couple of days earlier of how to get to the hostel from the station. So it was just kind of a sped up, a little walk, starting here, here's where you go. And I mean, that is very handy. I did, in fact, use it when the time came. That was nice. But then the second part of the video was, now here's what's going to happen when you get here. Even though again, this is just, this guy, I don't know, is just trying so hard to make it seem legit and fancy. So there's a panel you open outside as a front door, and it has a tablet on it. And you use the tablet with your code or with the QR thing. But that's not what gets you in. That gives you a second QR code that you have to scan with your phone. And you need to have this app called Sesame, this some kind of a lock app. And you got to make sure you've got the Wi-Fi password and everything ahead of time if you don't have data so that you can interface with the Sesame app. And once you scan that QR thing, then you can use the app and you just open the app and press unlock, and that unlocks the front door. And then inside the front door, you use the code you got at some point during this process to open a lock box that has the key to get into the room. And it's like, yeah, no fucking wonder this guy had reports that people were having trouble getting in. 45:00 What is this? Why are there so many steps that's insane? But it between that and his message of like, hey, I'm only going to be around till 10. And, you know, after that, if you aren't able to get in, just want to warn you, you know, like that could happen. I was motivated to get there before 10, you know what I'm saying now. Like I was pretty sure I followed all of his steps pretty well and I understood what was going to go on, but especially after the long journey and how exhausted I was and how by my old clock it was three or four in the morning, I was running on fumes and like the reason I picked this place was I remember when I booked it, I don't totally remember what was going through my mind because I really should have booked somewhere else even just like, why would I start off? Oh, I guess I know why. I guess I kind of expected that I would just get a lot of communal places because they were somewhat cheaper. So I knew this was like a communal thing, but I just really quickly as we go through this vacation all this roaming holiday, I'll describe all my Airbnb's as we go, but I pivoted quite quickly to realizing it's only a little bit more to get like private accommodations, sometimes whole like little mini apartments. I didn't necessarily need to do the communal thing, but I guess because this was the first one. And that's what I did by Naka Itabashi. That place was communal and it was great. I loved it.

So I remember thinking I guess when I booked this way back whenever I did, that I was kind of treating myself because of the location which hey, this has been great. All these places, these neighborhoods that I've never been to, I have fucking loved all these places including where I'm right now. And I'm still just on this cool little path. I don't actually think I might have got turned around this might not be the same path, but whatever. I'm going more or less the right way. And yeah, this is like, this has been great. But I remember I picked this place because it was central, you know, it's three stops away from Shibuya Station or one stop on the express.

Or in my terms, it was an hour long walk from Yoyogi Park. And it's like, okay, I know Yoyogi Park quite well from my last trip. So I could situate in my mind, I couldn't know where this place was. And even though my favorite places in Tokyo tend to not be central like this, I like the outskirts a lot. But Tokyo is so big, you know, like if I booked an Airbnb and some other weird obscure neighborhood, it would just take so much longer to be tacking on an extra two hours or so of confusing trains and things to try to get there. I just didn't want to do that when I first arrived. So I picked this place because I knew all trains lead to Shibuya. You know, it's almost impossible not to end up in Shibuya if you just ride random trains around. And from there, worst case scenario I could walk. So that's why I picked the place. And I mean that aspect of it did work out. It was good in my exhausted brain rattled state to have a place that was so easy to get to. But I'm getting ahead of myself. I'm still in the airport here at this point.

Okay, I'm still going the right way. Nice.

Unfortunately, I will have to turn off of this awesome little walking path before too long to head down to where I need to go. But yeah, I was in the airport. So even though in the moment, at the time, I felt like everything was pretty zany when I got into Tokyo. But by the next day already, it's like, I guess this is not that big a deal.

I definitely invent my own problems a lot in the moment. Man, this is fucking beautiful. I'm in a place called Meguro City now. And I just walked past like a fancy pants opera place, really tall building, all lit. I think it was beautiful people in tuxedos and crap walking around. So now I'm not there. I'm just some side streets and back streets. But this is significantly fancier overall than the other places I've been wandering around so far. And now I'm walking past a fine dining place that even has curtains that are just lowered enough that when my gocky face tries to look in as I walk by, you know, I'm not making eye contact with any of the patrons, but I can see all the fancy dishware, silk napkins and stuff.

I'm trying to set myself in a general direction. So as I wander, at least I'll sort of end up vaguely closer to get back to my terrible Airbnb. But that's really not possible. So arriving in Tokyo, I guess I was saying, yeah, I arrived in the airport and I went to the washroom and I got my first taste of Japan. When I visited before 2019, it's a little bit of a blur, but I think what they did is they just handed out custom forms to everybody on the plane and like a little pencil or a little pen. And then it was kind of an excessive amount of stuff to fill, and even though it's just no, no, no, no, no, no. Any weird vegetables? No. Bring it any guns or drugs in? No. Are you currently or have you ever been on the top 100 most wanted war criminals? CIA list? No. No. International incidents? No.

But this time I had kind of forgotten about this. I'm going to do it again. I'm going to go down a random side street. There's people coming my way on bicycles, so I mean, surely they came from somewhere, right? Maybe you just take a COVID test right there. I don't know. But I made sure I had all that stuff and all my paperwork and everything. You just walked through a second metal detector, but apparently it takes your temperature, and that was it. So second up, and I'd completely forgotten about this, was that in order to make that process a little faster, they started a thing called my Japan web. I think it's called where you could fill in all your vaccination stuff ahead of time. It wasn't until I went to check in for my WestJet flight the day before leaving that they were like, hey, by the way, go to this website, do this thing, and you'd get a QR code. Instead of having to go through all the paperwork at the airport, you can just do it now ahead of time and scan the QR code and you're good to go. I was just using my mom's. I had to look that up. Where are you staying when you get there? So I had to figure out the address of this hostel that is all in Japanese. I tried to translate it. Well, no, I guess based on, so I guess it was five a.m. for my hometown, but if I was in Montreal, to my body clock, it was four a.m. Whatever, who cares? It's not important. It was late. I was drinking coffee like a madman on the plane, and it was wearing off rapidly. 1:00:00 And I should have taken a nap on the plane, but I didn't, and I only slept, you know, five and a half hours. I was pleased with my sleep, but it really wasn't that much. So, I mean, I was, everything seemed worse because I was, you know, I'm running on fumes at this point. I'm like a puppet being held up by strings, you know. But the lineup, it was a super long line to talk to the immigration people, or the visa entry, whatever. I honestly don't know what this part of the airport process is called, but the line was going really fast.

And at one point, I had to give over my passport and take fingerprints. But then when I got to the guy, the QR thing didn't work, wouldn't scan. So he kicked me out and he said, go get a piece of paper. It's just like I was back on the plane again. I had to do the whole paper thing with the addresses and figuring it all out. Airbnb does not make it easy to figure out the phone number of the place, because I had to write that down too. What's the number of the place where you're staying? Through the mobile app, I had to literally try to phone the guy. It was the only way to see the number. And then luckily, because my phone isn't a phone, it didn't actually call him, it didn't work. But that's just, just annoying again, just a million little annoying things. And I like this neighborhood. This feels very different. This feels kind of like the stretch between Akihabra and Oeno.

Maybe it definitely feels like somewhere I've been before, but maybe that's not quite right.

So I slowly, painfully filled out the thing, went through the line again, I had to explain to the passport lady like I already just did this. This doesn't happen very often, but someone has to go through twice.

But that guy didn't even say a word, you know, just did some stuff on his end. I wrote down on the thing that I wanted to stay for the full 90 days. He just looked at my passport a bit, typed in some stuff, just gave me the approval stamp. Last time I was here, I mean, the guy talked to me, asked me some stuff, not this time, just honestly didn't care at all. So I was like, ah, sweet, alright, thank you.

Because even just stuff like the 90 days, I don't know, again, I'm coming up with stuff in my brain that no one else cares about. This guy's got, you know, hundreds of people, he's got a deal, but he doesn't care. But I feel like it is a little weird to stay for the whole 90, isn't it? I doubt most people do that. But again, just this assumption that anybody cares about me when they don't, in a good way. You know, it's good that they don't care. Just some guy. But then I got through there, and then it was the customs part where I was just confused, I didn't understand what was happening. Didn't know how to escape, I didn't know there was a next step. Finally figured out, you know, they gestured me to another even longer page where I had to fill out a lot of the same stuff. But even though I just filled it out, I would still have to do the whole process again of copying the Japanese address that I don't understand the formatting of and putting that into Google Translate and writing all stuff out. I started to do it, and I just, I was recognizing stuff, and I was like, wait a second. Both of the forms are somewhat redundant, but this was the longer one, and I'm like, man, this is the stuff I did. This is definitely the stuff that I did. So I went up to that guy with my QR code, and was like, hey, can we give this a try? I think this will work. And it did, and I was like, ah, suddenly it all became clear. Now I knew what had happened. Because I was so baffled that my QR code didn't work, because it told me it was fine. But it's because this was the wrong one. I was supposed to have two, I only had one. Not a lot of useful information in this podcast series, but that's definitely one of them. Make sure you get both QR codes. So they let me through. Now I was free. I was officially in Japan. But then, yeah, I got the message from the guy at the Airbnb, who put the fear of homelessness in me, you know. Because this all took so long that, you know, I thought arriving at 530, getting to the Airbnb by 10, I mean, everything is far away, in Tokyo, it's a big city. But I was not concerned, you know, I had lots of time. But the plane arrived late, I was in the airport forever. And I was like, oh my god.

Man, the confusingness of Tokyo, I feel like, I mean, my internal sense of direction is not good. But I feel like I'm walking in circles, like I've taken so many left turns that I'm going back where I came from. But according to my little map, I am actually getting closer to where I'm going. Because I guess it's just, again, because the roads aren't necessarily straight and stuff, so they're taking gentle curves here and there. 1:05:00 And my brain, for whatever little ability it does have to figure out what I'm doing, is only demarcating the turns when they're a direct 90 degree turn at intersection. All the other turns are going right over my subconscious's head.

So I got through Customs Proper, I was into the airport, and I was like, oh yeah, the first time I came, there's the first washroom I went to. So that is a spot of deep spiritual meaning to me. But instead of being excited, I was, again, just agitated and stressed. Because at this point, I don't know what time it was, I don't remember anymore, but I just knew that 10 o'clock thing. I mean, when you look at how far away Narita is, man, so far, my plan had been to take the normal train. But I think maybe I should take that expensive skyliner direct train, just to get there a little sooner.

But I couldn't figure out anything. I couldn't find a cash machine. I mean, let's just streamline all this, because who cares? I couldn't find an ATM to get money out, but I eventually did find it. And I took out $300 worth. 30,000 yen that was like the conversion rate to Canadian was like $290 some dollars. It was almost one to one. Which is a better rate than I had last time, but certainly not as vaunted as the my de-American dollar. Then the sweetca card, so it's sweetca, it just means watermelon, and it's just because the card is green. Got a green stripe on it, and a penguin. But I got this sweetca card in 2019, and I kept it ever since, just as a cool thing. Just keep it in my wallet, because I'm that guy. Just open my wallet and be like, ah, look at that, my little sweetca card from Japan. But it turns out it's kind of a handy thing that I kept it, because there's a shortage of them for whatever reason. I don't know why. So there's all these signs everywhere that were like, hey, if you're a visitor, you got to get this temporary sweetca visitor card. You can't get a real one anymore. But I still had mine, and my Montreal Metro card had run out, because it expires after four or five years or whatever, four years I think. Where I looked up this week a thing, and I think I read that it lasts for nine years, although you think ten would make more sense. But one of those, nine or ten. So theoretically mine should still be good. But again, there's just a lot of, ah, not understanding where to go, or what was happening. I found a lot of those signs and line-ups for the temporary card, but it was pretty tough to figure out where to refill my sweetca, but I finally found it. I've had $100 in there, and that was kind of cool. I put it on the little tray for to read my card, and it's still remembered that I had 160 yen on there. And that is cool, because I remember that my last day in Japan.

Me and my friend Brad went to a place called Osawa Falls that is so far west, it's hard to get much further west than that and still be in Tokyo. You gotta take the train all the way, then take a bus. I don't know if I'll actually make it back there this time because it's such a long journey. But it was pretty cool, but then the next morning had to leave, so I went all the way from there, all the way across Tokyo. It literally took all day. I left it like 7 in the morning. I got there somewhere in the afternoon. That was extra bad because it was not rush hour, so they were all local trains. No expressives. Every stop. I stopped at every fucking stop from Akigawa all the way to Nareta Airport. Ridiculous, but that's what I did that day. And that the money worked out so perfectly is wild. That I had the exact right amount of money on my suite card that I only had 100 and some yen left. I remember I'd forgotten about that. But I remember I felt proud about it at the time, like wow, it's like throwing a dart from a mile away and I did it. I didn't hit the bull's eye, but I hit the board. You know, good enough. So that was cool that it remembered. I still got my card. So mainly it's a subway card. But you can also use them at convenience stores and at some drink machines. So that's my main plan is to just use the card as much as possible and just have the money. As a backup. But yeah, kind of cool. That's like for the for the moment, just for a tiny moment, instead of feeling overwhelmed and stressed, because I couldn't figure out what was going on with anything. Just for a second, I was like, hey, this is cool. I'm the only one here. This gaggle of foreigners who has a real suite card. Awesome. Like why is everyone leaving all these lights on? This cost money, you know, turning off stuff when people weren't around or weren't using it. So you get up or make any movement whatsoever, all the lights turn on. But then you got to open the lock to leave this room to go use the washroom, for instance. And that was on a timer too. I was like, hey, I'm in Japan and everything's awesome. But, but I fucked up with this Airbnb. This place is ridiculous. 1:35:00 And as I was texting him, I looked down at my pant leg and I saw a bed bug.

And I wouldn't have known what it was except that we had a bed bug problem in New Brunswick as well. This tenant of my mom's. When she moved out, she left behind some bed bugs because she was like a dumpster diver, which I kind of appreciate that ethos, you know, that if people are just throwing stuff away and you have some use for it, yeah cool man, like an old table or something or whatever. But the dark side of it is that's how she got bed bugs in the house. And it was like a bit similar with that. When I saw bed bugs the first time, I saw the bed bug and I didn't know what it was. Just thought it was like a little beetle or something. And you know, we're from living in 100-year-old houses, rustic acid-landed Canada. I'm not bothered by bugs, you know, you share the world with the bugs you got to get used to them. So it's just like, hey there a little buddy of what, where do you go away. But then I saw another one. And then I started getting a weird feeling and I got like, wait a minute, what is this thing? And I researched it. And it was bed bugs. So this was definitely a bed bug. I know what these bastards look like. So I saw that one and I saw another one on like a curtain behind my bed. And I found two little teeny baby ones that were all gorge with blood. So it was just like, well there's the cherry on top of this joke. And again, this isn't this guy's fault, you know. This is like this sucks for him. Some dickhead brought bed bugs obviously. And I looked that up a bit. Like is this a thing in Japan? But I should be aware of. And mostly what I saw is that bed bugs are not a big problem in Japan, but they have been getting worse. And it's generally attributed to foreigners. And you know, there's stuff people will blame on foreigners that's not fair. But in this case, I feel like that's pretty safe to say is probably true, right? If people are just coming in from all over, you know shit they're bringing in bed bugs. Why wouldn't they? So my mission is just to make sure I do not bring them along with me. But the thing is, after that first night, then I didn't see any more in my little area. But then I saw one climbing up the little curtain outside. Someone else is bonking. It's just like, God, fucking dammit. Luckily though, I mean, you know, I'm just kind of getting used to this situation. I've started just like, stop, stop ingesting any liquids at sundown. So I don't have to pee too much at night. I've got my headphones in my earbuds with the rain noise at five. It's like my high volume. Five is the airport volume. But that's what I need to do to not hear everybody all night. In case you're wondering. Somebody left an umbrella here. I was like, I'm probably going to need an umbrella at some point. It's going to get rainier. But I do have a little collapsible one that's much crappier than this umbrella, but this one doesn't fold down. It's just kind of too big. What am I going to do with it?

I'll leave it here for someone else to take. But yeah, my mission is to just not bring the bed bugs with me. So every day when I leave, I check all my stuff extremely well. 1:40:00 When I get out of there, I'll again check extremely well, but the first thing I'm going to do with my next Airbnb, because this place has a washer and dryer, but he wants to charge money for it. Again, this is like fuck off. So first thing I'm going to do with the next place, put everything I own in the laundry, wash and dry, because the dryer in particular, the heat. Bed bugs aren't that hard to kill, and they can't fly, they can't jump, they're just idiots. They're not even resilient, like a flea, like you can just crush one with no trouble. Just rolling over in bed, you can accidentally kill them. That's what's so weird about them. They suck. They're such idiots. But they generally only come out when you're asleep. What are you going to do? They just dirtle out and they drink your blood and they leave. But also on the upside, since it's just a situation I can't do anything about, just got to accept it. Whereas for me, it's really just the psychological toll of just knowing that they're around, just knowing that as you're sleeping, they're crawling around. But the actual bites I don't feel them at all, I don't notice. Just thank God I'm only here for like five days. My old plan of like booking a place for weeks on end. Instead, this time it's a series of small trips just because that seems to be more the way of the world now with Airbnb. But this is a nice thing about that. But in my rattled sleep deprived, jet lagged, stressed out at my worst. All of this stuff was just like one after the other after the other after the other. And I was like, what the fuck? I mean this tissue paper bed bug ridden room with fucking tape over the lock outside. Stupid ass shit. Fuck. I was like this sucks.

But by the very next day, that's the podcast I did of my first day in Japan. Had a great goddamn time. Everything was fine the next day.

So that's the tale. That's the tale of me getting to Japan. I finally finally got it on wax. Finally got it out.

I guess again to reiterate the one piece of useful advice. Well, I mean in general, I guess is that these communal Airbnb's used to be a good deal. Now it's really not that much cheaper. I would not recommend it. I only did this because it was the first one I booked. I was kind of working under old assumptions. I didn't know the lay of the land yet, but I started to see how I could get whole units and whole little apartments to myself. The very least like a room for not very much more. There's really no need for this type of silliness.

The main appeal, the main reason anyone's willing to live in this tiny little shoe box with a zillion other people is just because of a location. And while the location is amazing, there's trains everywhere. I never had any trouble. You know, maybe when you get really far out when I was like way out past the Tamar River in Western Tokyo, yeah, okay, it was tough to get back in. But that's a severe example. Pretty much any outskirt neighborhood anywhere in greater Tokyo is fine. You'll have no trouble getting wherever you want to go. You don't need to be right there. It's not worth it. Don't do it. So that's the general advice. And the real advice is there's two QR codes when you do my visit Japan. Make sure you got both of them before you get to the airport. Because it's a little bit of a confusing website. I thought I was done. That wasn't done. All right, let us never speak of that again.

I'm almost back home. 5K, 5K is nothing. That was no biggie. Join me next time for an episode I already recorded earlier today about walking down the Maguro River. And it was fucking beautiful and great and gorgeous and I had a good time. So I'll see you there, Adios.
